title: Entangled Two-Photon Absorption in Cesium Atoms and the Limitations of the Far-Off-Resonance Approximation
authors: Michael Caracas Núñez, Dario Egloff, Mayerlin Nuñez Portela
abstract: The discrepancies between theoretical and experimental results in the process of entangled two-photon absorption (ETPA) are not fully understood. Atomic systems are a promising alternative for investigating this process without the systematic effects present in molecules. We present a theoretical study of the ETPA process in cesium atoms, focusing on the 6S1/2 --> 8S1/2 transition. The ETPA cross section is evaluated both with and without the far-off-resonance (FOR) approximation, including the contributions from intermediate atomic states and decoherence effects. The quantum state of light considered is described by the joint spectral amplitude of photon-pairs produced by the spontaneous parametric down-conversion process. When the FOR approximation is applied, the enhancement factor is constant (36*pi). In contrast, without this approximation the enhancement factor oscillates with the entanglement time, and achieves a maximum value of ~500. These results show the limitations of approximations when calculating the ETPA cross section and contribute to the understanding of the discrepancies between theoretical and experimental values for the ETPA cross section in different samples. The numbers presented in this work can be a starting point for designing experiments aimed at measuring ETPA cross sections in alkali atoms.
summary: This theoretical study calculates the entangled two-photon absorption cross-section in Cesium atoms using entangled photon pairs. It critically analyzes the limitations of the Far-Off-Resonance approximation, demonstrating that neglecting full spectral details causes the predicted enhancement factor to deviate significantly from the true, oscillating quantum value. The findings provide a crucial theoretical benchmark for future experimental measurements of ETPA.
main_problem: The paper investigates the discrepancies between theoretical and experimental results concerning the entangled two-photon absorption (ETPA) process in atomic systems.
main_result: The calculation shows that neglecting the Far-Off-Resonance (FOR) approximation leads to significant differences, with the enhancement factor oscillating and reaching values much higher than the constant value predicted by the FOR approximation.
method: The study employs a theoretical framework using the density operator formalism and calculates the ETPA cross-section by comparing results derived with and without the FOR approximation.
model_or_system: The system is Cesium atoms undergoing the $6S_{1/2} ightarrow 8S_{1/2}$ transition, driven by entangled photon-pairs generated via Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC).
key_observables: ETPA cross section ($\sigma_e$), enhancement factor ($F$), and the rate of ETPA ($R_{ETPA}$).
important_parameters: Entanglement time ($T_e$), Entanglement Area ($A_0$), and the detuning ($\Delta$) relative to the two-photon transition.
main_assumptions: The analysis critically compares results derived under the Far-Off-Resonance (FOR) approximation against the full calculation, which accounts for interference effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the ETPA cross-section dependence on detuning ($\Delta$) and entanglement time ($T_e$), explicitly comparing results obtained with and without the FOR approximation.
paper_structure: The paper builds the calculation by first defining the system using the joint spectral amplitude of the photon pairs, then deriving the cross-section using the density operator formalism, and finally contrasting the simplified FOR result against the full, oscillatory result.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it tackles a complex, non-linear quantum process (ETPA) and provides a rigorous comparison between simplified theoretical approximations and the full quantum treatment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Far-off-resonance ETPA cross section as a function of the detuning, ∆/2π, of the pump beam with respect to the two-photon transition 62S1/2 →82S1/2 in cesium atoms. The solid line corresponds to the total ETPA cross section, i.e, considering all the spectroscopic information in Appendix E. Dashed lines are the individual cross sections for each of the intermediate states. This graphic is obtained considering an entanglement time of Te = 100 fs and an entanglement area of A0 = 1.8 × 10−8 m2.

Figure 2. ETPA cross section without the far-off-resonance approximation as a function of the detuning ∆/2π for the TPA transition 62S1/2 →82S1/2 in cesium atoms. The inset shows the cross section for the intermediate transition paths with the lowest contributions to the total ETPA cross section.

Figure 3. ETPA cross section without the far-off-resonance approximation as a function of the entanglement time Te for ∆= 0 (resonance) for the TPA transition 62S1/2 →82S1/2 in cesium atoms. The inset shows the cross section for the intermediate transition paths with the lowest contributions to the total ETPA cross section.

Figure 5. Total rate of the TPA and ETPA processes as a function of the photon flux. The ETPA rate is computed from Eq. (40), while the TPA rate is computed from the numbers reported in [29]. For this calculation, we assumed an entan- glement area of A0 = 1.8 × 10−8 m2, an entanglement time Te = 95.44 fs, and an atomic density of 1019 atoms per m3. The inset shows the same plot with values around the photon flux required to have the same rates for the TPA and ETPA processes in logarithmic scale.

Figure 4. a) ETPA cross section as a function of the entan- glement time for the three approximations considered in this work. b) shows the enhancement factor F obtained without the FOR approximation as a function of the entanglement time Te.
